subject: ap-chemistry
unit_name: Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which molecule has a trigonal pyramidal geometry?

choices.explanation: $\text{BF}_3$ has 3 bonding pairs and 0 lone pairs around boron, giving trigonal planar geometry at 120°.
$\text{NH}_3$ has 4 electron domains (3 bonding + 1 lone pair), giving tetrahedral electron geometry but trigonal pyramidal molecular geometry with bond angles of ~107°.
$\text{CO}_2$ has 2 bonding domains (double bonds count as single domains), giving linear geometry at 180°.
$\text{CH}_4$ has 4 bonding pairs and 0 lone pairs, giving perfectly tetrahedral geometry at 109.5°.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: What is the bond angle in a water molecule ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$)?

choices.explanation: $180°$ is the bond angle for linear geometry (2 bonding domains, 0 lone pairs). $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ has 2 lone pairs.
$120°$ is trigonal planar geometry; $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ with 4 electron domains (2 bonding + 2 lone pairs) has a smaller angle.
$109.5°$ is ideal tetrahedral; $\text{H}_2\text{O}$'s 2 lone pairs compress the bond angle below this value.
$\text{H}_2\text{O}$ has bent geometry with 2 lone pairs strongly repelling the bonding pairs, compressing the H–O–H angle to approximately $104.5°$.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following molecules is nonpolar despite having polar bonds?

choices.explanation: $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ is bent (asymmetric), so dipole vectors do NOT cancel; it is a polar molecule.
$\text{NH}_3$ is trigonal pyramidal with one lone pair; dipole vectors don't cancel fully, making it polar.
$\text{CO}_2$ has two polar C=O bonds oriented linearly (180°); the dipole vectors are equal and opposite, canceling to give a net dipole of zero.
$\text{HCl}$ is diatomic with one polar bond; with only one bond there is nothing to cancel, so the molecule is polar.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: How many $\sigma$ (sigma) and $\pi$ (pi) bonds are in acetylene ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_2$), which has a triple bond?

choices.explanation: 3 $\sigma$ is correct (H–C, C≡C, C–H) but acetylene's triple bond contains 1 $\sigma$ + 2 $\pi$ bonds.
This undercounts — the two H–C single bonds each contribute 1 $\sigma$, plus the C–C $\sigma$, giving 3 total $\sigma$ bonds.
Each C–H is 1 $\sigma$ (×2 = 2 $\sigma$); the C≡C triple bond = 1 $\sigma$ + 2 $\pi$; total: 3 $\sigma$ + 2 $\pi$.
Acetylene only has 4 bonds total (3 $\sigma$ + 2 $\pi$) and cannot have 5 bonds.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: What hybridization is exhibited by the carbon atoms in benzene ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_6$)?

choices.explanation: $sp^3$ gives tetrahedral geometry; benzene's carbons form flat rings with 120° angles, characteristic of $sp^2$.
$sp$ hybridization gives linear geometry (180°); benzene's hexagonal ring requires 120° bond angles.
Each carbon in benzene forms 3 $\sigma$ bonds (to 2 carbons + 1 hydrogen) using $sp^2$ orbitals; the remaining unhybridized $p$ orbital participates in the delocalized $\pi$ system.
$sp^3d$ hybridization gives trigonal bipyramidal geometry; it's used for elements in Period 3+ with expanded octets, not carbon in benzene.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: The nitrate ion ($\text{NO}_3^-$) is best represented by resonance structures. What is the average bond order of each N–O bond?

choices.explanation: 1.0 would be a pure single bond; resonance delocalizes the double bond over 3 positions, giving fractional bond order.
The three resonance structures each have one N=O double bond and two N–O single bonds. Averaged: $(1 + 1 + 2)/3 = 4/3 \approx 1.33$.
2.0 would mean all three bonds are double bonds, which would require 24 valence electrons, more than the 24 available.
1.5 is the bond order in species with 2 resonance forms splitting one double bond over 2 positions, like $\text{NO}_2^-$, not $\text{NO}_3^-$.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: What are the formal charges on nitrogen and oxygen in the best Lewis structure of $\text{N}_2\text{O}$ (arranged N–N–O)?

choices.explanation: In the N=N=O structure, N(left) has FC = 5-2-3 = 0; this arrangement doesn't match.
In $:\text{N}\equiv\text{N}^+\!-\!\ddot{\text{O}}:^-$, terminal N = FC 5-2-3 = 0; central N = FC 5-0-4 = +1; O = FC 6-6-1 = –1. This minimizes formal charges and is the best structure.
With an odd number of total valence electrons (5+5+6=16), it's not possible to have all formal charges zero while satisfying the octet rule.
This assigns positive charge to the terminal N; the structure with the positive charge on the central atom (N≡N⁺–O⁻) is preferred because charges are minimized.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following correctly ranks the compounds by increasing lattice energy: $\text{NaF}$, $\text{MgO}$, $\text{NaCl}$?

choices.explanation: MgO has 2+ and 2− ions; its lattice energy is the highest, not the lowest.
Lattice energy increases with higher charge and smaller radius. NaCl (1+/1−, larger Cl−) < NaF (1+/1−, smaller F−) < MgO (2+/2−, much higher charge product).
NaF has a smaller Cl⁻ radius than NaCl (F⁻ < Cl⁻), so NaF has higher lattice energy than NaCl.
MgO with 2+/2− ions has far higher lattice energy than NaF with 1+/1− ions.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following species is an exception to the octet rule due to having an expanded octet?

choices.explanation: $\text{CO}_2$ satisfies the octet rule perfectly — each oxygen has 8 electrons and carbon has 8 electrons via two double bonds.
$\text{H}_2\text{O}$ satisfies the octet rule — oxygen has 8 electrons (2 bonds + 2 lone pairs).
$\text{BF}_3$ is an incomplete octet exception (boron has only 6 electrons), not an expanded octet.
$\text{SF}_6$ has 6 fluorine atoms bonded to sulfur, giving 12 electrons around S — an expanded octet possible because S is in Period 3 and can use $3d$ orbitals.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: In the Lewis structure of methane ($\text{CH}_4$), how many lone pairs of electrons are present on the central carbon atom?

choices.explanation: Carbon in $\text{CH}_4$ uses all 4 valence electrons to form 4 C–H bonds; there are no lone pairs on carbon.
Carbon has exactly 4 valence electrons, all used in C–H bonds; there are no electrons left for lone pairs.
If carbon had 2 lone pairs, it would only form 2 bonds; methane requires 4 bonds and has 0 lone pairs.
4 lone pairs would represent 8 extra electrons; carbon only has 4 valence electrons, all used in bonding.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Arrange the following bonds in order of increasing bond length: C–C, C=C, C≡C.

choices.explanation: This is the reverse; more bonds between atoms pull nuclei closer, decreasing bond length.
Bond length decreases as bond order increases; the triple bond (C≡C) is shortest (~120 pm), the double bond intermediate (~134 pm), and the single bond longest (~154 pm).
This incorrectly places the double bond shorter than the single bond but longer than the triple bond — the correct trend is triple < double < single.
This incorrectly places the single bond shorter than the double bond; single bonds are always longer than double bonds between the same atoms.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the geometry and polarity of $\text{XeF}_4$?

choices.explanation: Xe in $\text{XeF}_4$ has 4 bonding pairs + 2 lone pairs = 6 electron domains, giving octahedral electron geometry; the molecular geometry is square planar, not tetrahedral.

$\text{XeF}_4$ has 6 electron domains (4 bonds + 2 lone pairs) in an octahedral arrangement; the 2 lone pairs occupy opposite axial positions, giving square planar molecular geometry. The symmetric arrangement of F atoms causes all dipoles to cancel, making the molecule nonpolar.
Square pyramidal geometry has 5 bonds + 1 lone pair (like $\text{BrF}_5$); $\text{XeF}_4$ has 4 bonds + 2 lone pairs.
Octahedral is the electron-domain geometry, not the molecular geometry; with 2 lone pairs, the molecular shape is square planar.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: What is the shape of the $\text{ClF}_3$ molecule?

choices.explanation: Trigonal planar has 3 bonding + 0 lone pairs; $\text{ClF}_3$ has 3 bonding + 2 lone pairs = 5 electron domains.
$\text{ClF}_3$ has 5 electron domains (3 bonding + 2 lone pairs) in trigonal bipyramidal arrangement; the lone pairs occupy equatorial positions, giving a T-shaped molecular geometry.
Trigonal pyramidal has 4 electron domains (3 bonding + 1 lone pair); $\text{ClF}_3$ has 5 electron domains.
Bent geometry arises from 4 electron domains with 2 lone pairs (like $\text{H}_2\text{O}$); $\text{ClF}_3$ has 5 electron domains and a T-shaped geometry.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which bond is the most polar?

choices.explanation: The electronegativity difference for C–H is approximately $2.5 - 2.1 = 0.4$, which is very small (nearly nonpolar).
The electronegativity difference for N–H is approximately $3.0 - 2.1 = 0.9$.
The electronegativity difference for O–H is approximately $3.5 - 2.1 = 1.4$, which is polar.
The electronegativity difference for H–F is approximately $4.0 - 2.1 = 1.9$, the largest among the choices; F is the most electronegative element.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following bonds has the highest bond energy?

choices.explanation: The C–C single bond has the lowest bond energy of the choices; higher bond order correlates with higher bond energy.
The C=C double bond is stronger than the single bond but weaker than the triple bond.
The C≡N triple bond (in cyanide) has bond energy ~891 kJ/mol, the highest among these choices; triple bonds have the highest bond energy and shortest bond length.
C=O has a very high bond energy (~745 kJ/mol) due to the high electronegativity of O, but C≡N at ~891 kJ/mol is higher.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Metallic solids are excellent conductors of electricity because:

choices.text: Metal ions are free to move through the solid lattice
choices.explanation: Metal cations are fixed in the lattice; it is the electrons, not the ions, that are mobile in metallic solids.

choices.text: Valence electrons are delocalized and free to move throughout the lattice
choices.explanation: The 'sea of electrons' model describes delocalized valence electrons that can move freely in response to an electric field, enabling electrical conductivity.

choices.text: Metal atoms ionize completely and release electrons into solution
choices.explanation: This describes what happens when metals dissolve in acid, not how metallic solids conduct electricity in solid state.

choices.text: Covalent bonds throughout the metallic lattice allow electron flow
choices.explanation: Metallic bonding is NOT covalent bonding; covalent network solids (like diamond) are typically poor conductors.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: How many total valence electrons does $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$ (sulfate ion) have?

choices.explanation: S contributes 6, each of 4 O contributes 6 (= 24), plus 2 for the 2− charge: total = 6 + 24 + 2 = 32.
30 electrons would ignore the 2− charge; adding 2 electrons for the charge gives the correct total of 32.
$6 (\text{S}) + 4 \times 6 (\text{O}) + 2 (\text{charge}) = 6 + 24 + 2 = 32$ total valence electrons.
34 would correspond to a 4− charge or an extra pair of electrons beyond the correct total of 32.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following pairs shows a molecule and its INCORRECT hybridization?

choices.explanation: $\text{PCl}_5$ has 5 bonding domains, requiring trigonal bipyramidal geometry and $sp^3d$ hybridization — this is correct.
Ethylene's carbons each have 3 $\sigma$ bonds (2 C–H + 1 C–C) and one $\pi$ bond, consistent with $sp^2$ hybridization — correct.
Oxygen in $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ has 4 electron domains (2 bonds + 2 lone pairs), consistent with $sp^3$ hybridization — correct.
$\text{BeCl}_2$ has only 2 electron domains (2 bonding, 0 lone pairs), giving linear geometry and $sp$ hybridization — assigning $sp^3$ is incorrect.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes cis-trans (geometric) isomers in organic molecules?

choices.text: They require a single bond with restricted rotation
choices.explanation: Single bonds rotate freely; geometric isomerism requires restricted rotation, which occurs around double bonds, not single bonds.

choices.text: They have the same molecular formula but different connectivity of atoms
choices.explanation: This describes constitutional (structural) isomers; cis-trans isomers have the same connectivity but different spatial arrangement around a double bond.

choices.text: They have the same connectivity but different spatial arrangement around a double bond
choices.explanation: Cis-trans isomers share the same molecular formula and bond connectivity; they differ in whether identical substituents are on the same side (cis) or opposite sides (trans) of the double bond.

choices.text: They are non-superimposable mirror images of each other
choices.explanation: Non-superimposable mirror images are enantiomers (a type of stereoisomer involving chiral centers), not cis-trans isomers.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following represents an ionic compound?

choices.explanation: $\text{N}_2\text{O}$ (nitrous oxide) is a covalent compound formed between two nonmetals.
$\text{PCl}_3$ is a covalent compound; phosphorus and chlorine are both nonmetals.
$\text{CaCl}_2$ is ionic — calcium (metal) transfers electrons to chlorine (nonmetal), forming $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ and $2\text{Cl}^-$ ions held together by electrostatic attraction.
$\text{SO}_3$ (sulfur trioxide) is a covalent molecule formed between two nonmetals.
